name: Human Edge Commercial
description: Evaluates thinking quality + trust impact across 5 dimensions. Composite 5–10 = Replaceable (high AI risk); 11–18 = Transitional (at risk); 19–25 = Human Edge (differentiated, low AI risk).

criteria.name: Rigor — Thinking Depth
criteria.description: Depth of analysis: does the rep reframe the problem or stay on the surface?

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Surface-level, reactive. Accepts the client's framing without probing.

levels.label: Below Average
levels.description: Occasional probes but mostly takes surface answers at face value.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Some structure and limited probing. Asks follow-ups but doesn't pressure-test.

levels.label: Good
levels.description: Structured analysis with meaningful probes that test assumptions.

levels.label: Excellent
levels.description: Deep analysis that reframes the problem — client sees their situation in a new way.

criteria.name: Relevance — Application
criteria.description: How well the rep contextualizes everything to this customer's specific reality, not a generic pitch.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Generic, product-first. Same pitch regardless of audience.

levels.label: Below Average
levels.description: Minor tailoring, mostly boilerplate with surface references to the client.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Some tailoring to industry or role.

levels.label: Good
levels.description: Tailored to the stakeholder's role, KPIs, and specific context.

levels.label: Excellent
levels.description: Fully contextualized to the customer's reality — feels built for them and their situation.

criteria.name: Trust — Credibility
criteria.description: Expertise demonstrated in context. Does the rep earn the right to be taken seriously?

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Sounds scripted or shallow. Cannot answer substantive questions.

levels.label: Below Average
levels.description: Some expertise visible but inconsistent; leans on marketing language.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Competent but standard — could be any rep at any vendor.

levels.label: Good
levels.description: Clear subject expertise demonstrated in context with specific examples.

levels.label: Excellent
levels.description: Demonstrates real expertise that reframes the client's understanding of the space.

criteria.name: Trust — Intimacy
criteria.description: Does the customer open up? Is there real dialogue vs transactional Q&A?

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: No personal connection. Transactional exchange only.

levels.label: Below Average
levels.description: Transactional warmth — pleasant but shallow.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Basic rapport established. Polite and professional.

levels.label: Good
levels.description: Client shares real concerns and priorities beyond the official brief.

levels.label: Excellent
levels.description: Customer opens up — real dialogue. They share things they hadn't planned to share.

criteria.name: Trust — Self-Orientation (inverted)
criteria.description: Scored inverted — 5 = customer-first, 1 = pitch-focused. In the Trust Equation, Self-Orientation is the denominator: lower is better. Higher scores on this criterion mean lower self-orientation.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Pitch-focused. Drives own agenda, ignores client signals, talks over customer.

levels.label: Below Average
levels.description: Agenda-driven with occasional nods to the customer's situation.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Balanced — roughly equal attention to own agenda and customer needs.

levels.label: Good
levels.description: Mostly customer-oriented. Occasional agenda moments but quickly returns to customer.

levels.label: Excellent
levels.description: Clearly customer-first. Every move serves the customer's outcome, not the rep's quota.
